questions.question_text: Who is responsible for the safekeeping of earnest money in a Utah transaction?
distractors: The sales agent who collected it
The title company at all times
The buyer until closing
questions.explanation: The principal broker is responsible for the safekeeping of earnest money. It must be deposited per contract terms or Division rules into the broker's trust account.

questions.question_text: A transaction falls through and both the buyer and seller claim the earnest money. The broker should:
questions.correct_answer: Hold the funds until written agreement or court order
distractors: Split the funds equally between parties
Return the funds to the buyer automatically
Release the funds to whoever asks first
questions.explanation: When a dispute arises over earnest money, the broker must not release funds until receiving written agreement from all parties or a court order. Interpleading with the court is an option if parties cannot agree.

questions.question_text: Both parties in a failed transaction refuse to agree on earnest money distribution. The broker may:
questions.correct_answer: Interplead the funds with the court
distractors: Keep the funds as a brokerage fee
Release them to the seller by default
Donate them to the Recovery Fund
questions.explanation: Interpleading funds with the court is an option when the parties cannot agree on distribution. The court then determines the proper disposition of the disputed earnest money.

questions.question_text: The terms for earnest money deposit timing in a Utah transaction are determined by:
questions.correct_answer: The REPC contract terms or Division rules
distractors: The title company's internal policy
A standard 48-hour state deadline
The buyer's lender requirements
questions.explanation: Earnest money must be deposited according to the terms of the REPC contract or within the time specified by Division rules. There is no universal fixed deadline; it depends on the contract.

questions.question_text: A sales agent holds an earnest money check in their desk for a week before giving it to the principal broker. This is:
questions.correct_answer: A violation of prompt deposit requirements
distractors: Acceptable if the amount is under $5,000
Standard practice for new agents
Permitted until the inspection period ends
questions.explanation: Trust funds must be deposited promptly into the principal broker's trust account. Holding checks instead of delivering them to the PB promptly violates trust fund handling requirements.

distractors: Set by the Division of Real Estate
Fixed at 6% for residential properties
Determined by the Real Estate Commission
questions.explanation: Commission rates are negotiable in Utah. There is no standard or fixed rate. The Division does not set commission schedules, and any agreement on rates must be between the parties.

questions.question_text: A sales agent closes a deal and expects to receive their commission. Who pays the agent?
distractors: The seller directly at closing
The buyer through the title company
The Division from the Recovery Fund
questions.explanation: Only the principal broker can receive compensation for a real estate transaction. Sales agents and associate brokers receive their share through the PB, never directly from clients or other parties.

questions.question_text: A principal broker fails to pay a cooperating broker the agreed commission. Who has standing to sue?
distractors: The cooperating sales agent directly
The buyer who paid the commission
The Division on behalf of the broker
questions.explanation: Only a licensed principal broker can bring a lawsuit to collect a commission. Individual sales agents or associate brokers cannot sue for commission; their recourse is through their own PB.

questions.question_text: A seller agrees to pay a 5% commission but later claims the standard rate should be 6%. The broker's commission is:
questions.correct_answer: 5% as agreed in the listing agreement
distractors: 6% as the standard rate
Whatever the Real Estate Commission sets
Subject to Division arbitration
questions.explanation: Commission rates are negotiable and governed by the listing agreement. Since 5% was agreed upon, that is the enforceable rate. There is no 'standard rate' in Utah.

questions.question_text: An associate broker working under a principal broker wants to receive commission directly from the seller at closing. This is:
questions.correct_answer: Prohibited; commission goes through the PB
distractors: Allowed with seller's written consent
Permitted for associate brokers only
Standard for experienced licensees
questions.explanation: Sales agents and associate brokers receive their compensation through the principal broker, never directly from clients. Only the PB can receive transaction compensation.

questions.question_text: A licensee pays a $500 referral fee to an unlicensed friend who sent them a client. This is:
distractors: Acceptable for amounts under $1,000
Legal if documented as a gift only
Permitted as a marketing expense
questions.explanation: Referral fees may only be paid to licensed persons in Utah. Paying referral fees to unlicensed individuals is prohibited regardless of the amount or how it is characterized.

questions.question_text: A buyer's agent also receives a referral fee from the seller's agent for bringing a buyer. What must occur?
questions.correct_answer: Both parties must be informed of the dual compensation
distractors: Nothing; referral fees are private matters
The Division must approve the payment
The fee must be deposited in the trust account
questions.explanation: If an agent receives compensation from more than one party, this must be disclosed to all parties in the transaction. Transparency about compensation is required.

questions.question_text: An agent who owns rental property wants to list their own property for sale. They must disclose:
questions.correct_answer: Their personal interest in the transaction
distractors: Only the fact that they hold a license
Nothing beyond what sellers normally disclose
The property's rental income history only
questions.explanation: Licensees must disclose any personal interest in a transaction. Acting as both the seller and the listing agent requires full disclosure to potential buyers.

questions.question_text: A sales agent refers a client to another brokerage in a different city and receives a referral fee. This is:
questions.correct_answer: Permitted since both parties are licensed
distractors: Prohibited for out-of-state referrals
Only allowed with Division approval
Illegal if the fee exceeds $1,000
questions.explanation: Referral fees between licensed persons are permitted. The key requirement is that the recipient holds a valid real estate license. The fee goes through the principal brokers, not directly between agents.

questions.question_text: Which statement about Utah compensation disclosure is correct?
questions.correct_answer: All sources of compensation must be disclosed
distractors: Only buyer compensation must be disclosed
Disclosure is optional for co-op commissions
Compensation details are confidential
questions.explanation: If an agent receives compensation from more than one party, this must be disclosed to all parties. Transparency about all compensation sources is a fundamental obligation in Utah real estate practice.
